metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the minimum verifiable content for the `docs/releasing.md` pinning section: name required headings and at least one concrete proving check (e.g. grep for `pip-compile --generate-hashes` command presence, or list the subsections that must exist: 'Pinning Policy', 'Updating Action SHAs', 'Updating Build-Tool Hashes', 'Updating the MCP Publisher Pin').
findings.finding: The `docs/releasing.md` AC ('documents the pinning policy and pin-update process') states no observable content requirement — no required headings, no minimum content checklist, no concrete proving command — making it impossible to verify done-ness in-session beyond 'the file exists and has some text'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `docs/releasing.md` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC with: '`docs/releasing.md` contains a "Pinning" section with at minimum: (a) the rationale for full-SHA action pins, (b) the command to regenerate the lock file (`pip-compile --generate-hashes …`), and (c) instructions for updating the MCP publisher pin — verified by `grep -q "pip-compile --generate-hashes" docs/releasing.md` in the lint step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace every occurrence of `--no-build-isolation` (What section, AC4, lint step) with `--no-isolation`, which is the actual pypa/build CLI flag that disables PEP 517 build isolation.
findings.finding: The plan names `python -m build --no-build-isolation` as the build invocation in both the 'What' section and AC4, and requires a lint step that 'fails if `--no-build-isolation` is removed'. The actual pypa/build CLI flag for disabling build isolation is `--no-isolation`, not `--no-build-isolation`. The lint grep would never match the real flag, leaving the enforcement gap the AC is intended to close.
findings.location: What section ('--no-build-isolation' in the Hatchling enforcement decision); AC4 ('fails if `--no-build-isolation` is removed')
findings.suggested_fix: In both the What section and AC4, change `python -m build --no-build-isolation` to `python -m build --no-isolation`, and update the lint grep accordingly to check for `--no-isolation`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct `python -m build --no-build-isolation` to `python -m build --no-isolation` (the actual pypa/build CLI flag) in the What section and throughout AC4, and update the AC4 lint check to grep for `--no-isolation` rather than `--no-build-isolation`.
findings.finding: The plan names `python -m build --no-build-isolation` throughout (What section and AC4), but the `pypa/build` CLI flag for skipping build isolation is `--no-isolation` (short `-n`), not `--no-build-isolation`. The `--no-build-isolation` flag belongs to `pip install`. Using the wrong flag on `python -m build` will cause it to reject the option with an unrecognised-argument error, or in permissive versions silently ignore it — either outcome defeats the Hatchling-version enforcement the plan depends on. The AC4 lint check also searches for the literal string `--no-build-isolation` in the workflow, so if an implementer uses the correct `--no-isolation` flag, the lint step would FAIL (false positive), and vice versa.
findings.location: What section ('python -m build --no-build-isolation') and AC4: 'Hatchling's version is enforced end-to-end via `--no-build-isolation`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace every occurrence of `python -m build --no-build-isolation` with `python -m build --no-isolation`. In AC4, change 'a workflow lint/test step that fails if `--no-build-isolation` is removed' to 'fails if `--no-isolation` is absent from the build invocation'. Verify by running `python -m build --help` and confirming `-n`/`--no-isolation` is the isolation-disable flag.

findings.finding: The plan consistently names `python -m build --no-build-isolation` (What section and AC4), but the `pypa/build` CLI flag for disabling build isolation is `--no-isolation`, not `--no-build-isolation`. `--no-build-isolation` is a `pip install` flag. Using the wrong flag will cause `python -m build` to reject the option with an unrecognised-argument error (or silently pass through in some versions, bypassing the intended enforcement entirely).
findings.location: What section ('python -m build --no-build-isolation') and AC: 'Hatchling's version is enforced end-to-end via `--no-build-isolation`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace every occurrence of `python -m build --no-build-isolation` in the plan (What section + AC4) with `python -m build --no-isolation`. Update the AC4 lint/grep check to search for `--no-isolation` in the workflow. Note: `pip install --no-build-isolation` is the correct spelling for pip, so keep that wording only where pip is the subject.

findings.finding: The plan's SHA-pin task requires resolving `actions/upload-artifact@v7` (release.yml:43) and `actions/download-artifact@v8` (release.yml:60,85) to 40-char commit SHAs, but these major-version tags (`v7`, `v8`) do not correspond to any published releases of these GitHub Actions — the latest published major for both is v4. If these tags cannot be resolved to a real commit SHA, the SHA-pinning task is implementation-blocked for those steps.
findings.location: Scope section ('SHA-pin all `uses:` in release.yml'); release.yml lines 43, 60, 85
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section that implementation must first update `actions/upload-artifact` and `actions/download-artifact` to their current published major versions (v4 as of 2025) before resolving them to commit SHAs, since the currently referenced tags (v7, v8) do not exist in the upstream action repositories.

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the positive path of the new `mcp_verify → mcp_registry` two-job flow end-to-end as it will run in production (i.e., successful download + verify + upload artifact in `mcp_verify`, followed by `mcp_registry` consuming the artifact and performing login/publish). AC5 only specifies a negative/corruption test. Under E6(f), a cutover to a new code path requires at least one AC that exercises the live path end-to-end; the plan does not address this gap or explicitly defer it with rationale.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'Verified by a CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits non-zero.'

findings.finding: The AC for the MCP publisher step requires 'a CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive, runs the verify step, and asserts it exits non-zero' as the verification mechanism, but this test job is never described or scoped in the 'What' or 'Scope' sections — it is an orphan verification artifact with no implementation guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — MCP publisher AC ('Verified by a CI job that corrupts one byte...'); Scope section (no corresponding entry)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section under 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute', add a fourth bullet describing the negative test: e.g., 'A CI lint/test step (e.g. `.github/scripts/test-mcp-verify.sh`) runs on every PR touching the verify logic: it downloads the archive, corrupts one byte with `dd`, and asserts `sha256sum -c` exits non-zero.' Add this script/step to the Scope file list.

findings.finding: AC7 ('`docs/releasing.md` documents the pinning policy and pin-update process') names a deliverable but provides no concrete proving command — no grep/check is specified that would produce a pass/fail signal in CI or in a review. A reviewer cannot deterministically confirm the required sections exist without an explicit check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the workflow-lint step to run: `grep -qE '^## Pinning' docs/releasing.md || (echo 'Missing Pinning section in docs/releasing.md' && exit 1)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the corruption-test CI job (or dedicated test script) as an explicit scope deliverable — name the file/location (e.g. `.github/workflows/test-mcp-verify.yml` or a `make test-mcp-verify` target) and describe its inputs/outputs so an agent knows what to build.
findings.finding: The MCP publisher digest-verification AC names a CI job that corrupts one byte of the pinned archive and asserts non-zero exit as the proving mechanism, but this corruption-test job does not appear anywhere in the Scope section as a deliverable. An executing agent following the scope would implement the `mcp_verify`/`mcp_registry` split but would have no instruction to build the adversarial test job, leaving the AC un-provable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — MCP publisher bullet; Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope bullet such as: '`.github/workflows/test-mcp-verify.yml` (or `scripts/test_mcp_verify.sh`) — a self-contained test that downloads the pinned archive, corrupts one byte, runs the `sha256sum -c` verify step, and asserts exit code ≠ 0; executed by the Gerrit gate on changes touching `release.yml`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `mcp_verify` must have `needs: publish` (or `mcp_registry` must have `needs: [mcp_verify, publish]`) so the PyPI-first ordering that the existing `mcp_registry` job relies on is preserved in the split.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a `mcp_verify → mcp_registry` job split but does not specify that the new `mcp_registry` must retain `needs: publish` from the current implementation. The current job has `needs: publish`; the plan only states `mcp_registry` should have `needs: mcp_verify`. Without also keeping `needs: publish` (or chaining `mcp_verify` after `publish`), the OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job could race ahead of the PyPI publish job, causing the registry's PyPI-existence check to 404.
findings.location: What section (MCP publisher — structural job split); Scope section ('split the MCP publisher into an unprivileged `mcp_verify` job + an OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the job-split spec: '`mcp_verify` has `needs: publish`; `mcp_registry` has `needs: mcp_verify`' — this preserves the PyPI-first ordering while achieving the structural separation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the '/ sets a `verified` output' alternative in both the What section and AC 5, leaving only the artifact upload/download path as the specified hand-off mechanism from `mcp_verify` to `mcp_registry`, so the OIDC-privileged job is structurally unable to download the binary.
findings.finding: The 'What' section and AC 5 include the clause 'uploads the verified binary as an artifact / sets a `verified` output' — the OR escape hatch ('/ sets a `verified` output') allows an implementation where `mcp_registry` re-downloads the mcp-publisher binary from the internet inside the OIDC-privileged job, using the `verified` output only as a boolean signal. This re-creates the exact co-residency risk the job split is designed to prevent: the download (and potential compromise) now occurs inside the job that holds `id-token: write`.
findings.location: What section (MCP publisher — 'Security contract — structural job split') and AC 5
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section replace 'uploads the verified binary as an artifact / sets a `verified` output' with 'uploads the verified binary as a GitHub Actions artifact'. In AC 5 replace the same clause with 'uploads the verified mcp-publisher binary as a named GitHub Actions artifact; `mcp_registry` downloads that artifact (never re-downloads from the internet) and executes it'. This closes the escape hatch while keeping the mechanism clear.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `persist-credentials: false` exception: either (a) remove the 'unless' qualifier from the What section and confirm no release step needs the persisted credential, OR (b) add a lint-suppressible exception mechanism to AC2 (e.g. 'fail unless an explicit comment documents why the credential is needed') — so the described approach and the AC/lint agree.
findings.finding: The What section permits an exception to `persist-credentials: false` ('unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential'), but AC2 is absolute ('Every release checkout sets `persist-credentials: false`') with a lint that fails if absent — no exception path exists in the AC or the lint. An implementer who encounters a checkout that genuinely needs the credential (e.g. a push step) is sent in two directions: the What section says omit the flag in that case; the AC and lint say it must be present.
findings.location: AC2 vs. What section ('Set `persist-credentials: false` on every release checkout unless…')
findings.suggested_fix: If no current release step needs a persisted credential, drop the 'unless' clause from the What section entirely and keep the absolute AC. If a step might need it, add to AC2: 'fail unless a `# persist-credentials: needed because <reason>` comment is present adjacent to the checkout block' — giving the lint an auditable override path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether modelcontextprotocol/registry releases include a checksums.txt (or equivalent) before the plan is finalized, and record the result so the implementer knows which verification path to use.
findings.finding: The plan makes an unverified capability assertion about the MCP publisher upstream: 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256.' The plan does not verify whether modelcontextprotocol/registry actually ships a checksums.txt with its releases, leaving the implementer to discover at implementation time which of the two paths applies — and potentially assuming the preferred path exists when it does not.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute'; 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one'
findings.suggested_fix: Check https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases and inspect the asset list for a recent release. Record the finding (e.g., 'as of vX.Y.Z, no checksums.txt is published; SHA-256 is hard-coded') as a comment in the plan and in the workflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default path for the lock file (e.g. `requirements/release-tools.txt` or `.github/release-tools.txt`) rather than leaving it as an open 'e.g.' choice.
findings.finding: The lock/constraints file location is specified only as an example ('e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`'), leaving the agent to make an unguided structural choice that affects other scope items (the install command path, documentation, the lint step's reference path).
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file' bullet; Scope — 'A new release build lock/constraints file (e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`).'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'e.g. under `.github/` or `requirements/`' with a decided path such as `requirements/release-tools.txt`, and use that exact path in the `--require-hashes` install command and the `docs/releasing.md` pin-update instructions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a workflow-lint AC (alongside the existing SHA-pin grep) that fails if `mcp_registry` contains any `curl`/`wget` download step OR lacks a `needs: mcp_verify` declaration — so the structural OIDC-isolation invariant is enforced by the same CI gate as the SHA-pin checks.
findings.finding: The security claim that 'the OIDC scope is never co-resident with an unverified binary' depends entirely on the structural job split (mcp_registry has no download step and declares `needs: mcp_verify`), but no AC or test verifies this structural invariant. The corruption-detection CI job only proves sha256sum exits non-zero on a bad archive; it does not prevent a future contributor from silently adding a `curl` download step directly to mcp_registry — restoring the vulnerability while all existing tests continue to pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 5 ('Verified by a CI job that corrupts one byte…'); 'What' section — 'Security contract — structural job split'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the existing workflow-lint/grep step with two additional checks: (1) `grep -P 'curl|wget' release.yml` applied only to lines within the `mcp_registry` job block must return non-zero; (2) the `mcp_registry` job block must contain `needs: mcp_verify` or `needs: [mcp_verify, …]`. Add these as explicit pass/fail assertions to the lint script and document them in AC 5 alongside the corruption-detection test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend the `docs/releasing.md` scope to include updating the 'Supply-chain attestations' section's `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` reference to the pinned SHA, or explicitly note this pre-existing reference as out-of-scope with a follow-up.
findings.finding: The plan claims ownership of `docs/releasing.md` for a 'Pinning' section only, but an existing section — 'Supply-chain attestations' (lines 44–45) — hardcodes the mutable `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` tag verbatim. After this story SHA-pins that action in `release.yml`, the docs reference becomes stale and contradictory. This is an unenumerated consumer of the action-pin change that lies outside the plan's stated section ownership.
findings.location: Scope section ('docs/releasing.md — document the pinning policy and the pin-update process. Section ownership: this story owns the "Pinning" section only'); docs/releasing.md lines 44–45

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate zizmor and/or actionlint as the enforcement mechanism for SHA-pin and persist-credentials checks before committing to a bespoke grep/workflow-lint step; document the decision (adopt the established tool, or explicitly justify why a custom grep is preferred) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke 'workflow-lint/grep check' to enforce SHA-pin and persist-credentials requirements in release.yml, reinventing what established tools (zizmor, actionlint) already provide. Zizmor is a dedicated, actively maintained GitHub Actions supply-chain security static analyzer that flags unpinned action refs, missing persist-credentials:false, excessive permissions, and related issues — exactly the checks the plan hand-rolls. The plan shows no awareness of these tools and does not justify the custom implementation over the established alternatives.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('verified by a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check'); AC2 ('verified by the same workflow-lint step'); What section — 'Replace each action tag with a full commit SHA'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bespoke grep step with `zizmor --pedantic .github/workflows/release.yml` (or `actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml`) in CI. Both tools are installable as a single binary with no runtime deps. If a bespoke check is retained for any reason (e.g., to enforce a project-specific comment format), document the gap zizmor doesn't cover.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `mcp_registry` re-verifies the SHA-256 digest of the mcp-publisher binary it downloads from artifact storage (using the same pinned hash) before executing it, ensuring cryptographic enforcement at the consumption point, not only at the upload point.
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job consumes the mcp-publisher binary from GitHub Actions artifact storage (uploaded by `mcp_verify`) but the plan never specifies a re-digest check in `mcp_registry` before executing the binary. An adversary with write access to the artifact store (e.g., a malicious concurrent workflow run in the same repo) could substitute the artifact between upload and download, bypassing the sha256sum verification performed in `mcp_verify` and allowing an unverified binary to execute with `id-token: write` in scope.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; AC5 — 'mcp_registry job needs: mcp_verify and consumes only the verified artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: After the `actions/download-artifact` step in `mcp_registry`, add: `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` — the same pinned hash used in `mcp_verify`. Document this as the terminal enforcement point: even if artifact storage is compromised, the OIDC-privileged execution context never runs an unverified binary.

findings.finding: The plan directs multiple jobs (`mcp_verify`, the build job, and the SHA-pin lint step) to each 'append' to a single shared `release-evidence.txt` artifact, but GitHub Actions artifact uploads are not atomic append operations — concurrent or sequential uploads with the same artifact name conflict or overwrite each other, leaving the assembly mechanism undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 6 (release evidence artifact); What section — 'Print and retain in release evidence'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out / rollback procedure for the `--no-build-isolation` change (e.g. 'to revert: remove the lint step, restore `pip install --upgrade build` and `python -m build`, open a tracking issue') in `docs/releasing.md` or a plan note — satisfying T4's requirement that destructive/irreversible steps have a described reversibility mechanism, not just a justification.
findings.finding: The `--no-build-isolation` lint-gate is explicitly designed as a one-way lock ('A workflow lint/test step that fails if `--no-build-isolation` is removed — no free-resolution path remains'), but no rollback or back-out path is described for the case where this change causes build failures (e.g. lock file incompatibility, Hatchling build-system plugin incompatibility in the pre-installed env). The plan justifies the choice but provides no reversibility mechanism, which T4 requires beyond mere acknowledgement.
findings.location: What section — '--no-build-isolation' subsection; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/releasing.md under the Pinning section, add a 'Rolling back the hash-locked build environment' subsection describing: (1) the steps to temporarily disable the lint gate, (2) how to restore the pre-pinning build commands, and (3) the process for re-pinning once the incompatibility is resolved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--max-time 60 --connect-timeout 10` (or equivalent) to the `curl` download step and set a `timeout-minutes` on the `mcp_verify` job so that a hung download fails fast rather than blocking the release pipeline for hours.
findings.finding: The `curl` download of the MCP publisher archive has no timeout specified; if the GitHub releases endpoint is slow or unresponsive the `mcp_verify` job will hang indefinitely, blocking the entire release pipeline with no timeout or circuit-breaker.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download step 1: `curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`
findings.suggested_fix: In the `mcp_verify` job definition add `timeout-minutes: 10`, and change the curl invocation to `curl -fsSL --max-time 60 --connect-timeout 10 "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in the plan and in `docs/releasing.md`) whether the MCP registry publish operation is idempotent on retry, and specify the recovery procedure (or a pre-publish existence check) for the case where `mcp-publisher publish` fails after partial state has been written to the registry.
findings.finding: The plan does not address MCP publisher publish-step idempotency or partial-publish recovery: if `mcp-publisher publish` succeeds partially (e.g., artifact uploaded but not indexed) and the workflow is re-run, the behavior of a second publish attempt against the registry API is unspecified, risking a corrupted or duplicate release state.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher split job design; `mcp_registry` job execution of `mcp-publisher login`/`publish`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (adding a `_RELEASE_YML` path + a new test asserting all `uses:` in `release.yml` match a 40-hex SHA pattern and every `actions/checkout` block contains `persist-credentials: false`) instead of introducing a new bespoke workflow-lint/grep step — the existing `_read()` + `assert … in content` pattern already handles this with zero new infrastructure.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new bespoke 'workflow-lint/grep check' step to enforce SHA-pin and persist-credentials requirements in release.yml, rebuilding capability already present in tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py, which uses the identical mechanism (text-search workflow YAML files via _read() + assert sig in content) with 3+ existing call sites enforcing workflow properties (pip-audit, pre-commit, module-size, etc.).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC2 ('verified by a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check'); also What section ('A workflow lint/test step fails if `--no-build-isolation` is absent').
findings.suggested_fix: Add to test_ci_workflow_parity.py: import re; _RELEASE_YML = _ROOT / '.github' / 'workflows' / 'release.yml'; test that re.findall(r'uses: [^\n]+', release_text) all match r'uses: \w[\w/-]+@[0-9a-f]{40}\b', and that every 'actions/checkout' block contains 'persist-credentials: false'. This reuses the existing test infrastructure and is automatically gated by both test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml without any new CI step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare `permissions: {}` (or the minimal required set, e.g. `contents: read` only) for the `mcp_verify` job in the plan spec and AC5, so the no-`id-token` constraint is enforced structurally and not silently inherited.
findings.finding: The plan never declares the `permissions:` block for the new `mcp_verify` job. Without an explicit declaration, the job inherits the workflow-level `permissions: contents: read` (release.yml line 23–24), which is acceptable — but the plan's least-privilege contract for the job split depends on `mcp_verify` having no `id-token: write`, and this constraint is not stated in the spec text or ACs, leaving it silently reliant on the workflow-level default rather than an explicit, enforced minimum.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; Scope section — 'split the MCP publisher into an unprivileged mcp_verify job'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `mcp_verify` job definition in the plan: `permissions: contents: read  # explicitly no id-token: write — this is the enforcement boundary` and add a corresponding AC check (grep for `id-token` in the `mcp_verify` job block and fail if present).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The repo already SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml` (per its header comment)' and bases the AC lint on this being an extendable existing convention. However, `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 75 contains `actions/setup-python@v6` — a tag pin, not a SHA — directly contradicting the header comment ('All third-party actions are SHA-pinned'). The existing SHA-pin coverage is only partial (non-`actions/*` third-party actions like `lfreleng-actions/*` and `im-open/*` are SHA-pinned; `actions/setup-python@v6` is not). The AC's lint is therefore not 'extendable' from a complete existing convention — it must be newly created, and the gap in `gerrit-verify.yaml` itself is not acknowledged or scoped.
findings.location: Why section ('The repo already SHA-pins third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`') and AC line 1 ('extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml`')
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, replace 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in `gerrit-verify.yaml`' with 'a new workflow-lint grep step targeting `release.yml` (note: `gerrit-verify.yaml` line 75 also has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` — include or explicitly exclude that fix from this story's scope).'  In the Why section, narrow the premise to 'The repo SHA-pins some third-party actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`' rather than asserting it is comprehensive.

findings.finding: The AC's post-install verification mechanism (`pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>`) does not actually verify that already-installed packages' wheel digests match the lock file: pip marks packages 'already satisfied' and skips digest-checking them during --dry-run, so a tampered or wrong-source pre-installed package at a matching version would silently pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 3: 'pip install --dry-run --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock> and asserts it exits zero (every installed version satisfies the locked hashes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove --dry-run from the verification command: `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r <lock>` in an already-installed environment will either re-install and hash-verify (if hashes match, it is idempotent) or fail if a package was replaced. Alternatively, add a dedicated audit step: for each package in the lock, compute the installed wheel's hash with `pip show -f <pkg>` + sha256 of the RECORD-listed .dist-info/WHEEL file and compare against the lock's expected hash.

findings.finding: The AC for the SHA-pin enforcement check states it is 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in gerrit-verify.yaml', implying an existing automated check that can be extended. No such automated lint/grep check exists — the 'convention' is a prose header comment only (gerrit-verify.yaml:21). The plan must create this check from scratch; framing it as 'extendable' may mislead the implementor into searching for an existing script.
findings.location: AC line 1 — 'verified by a workflow-lint/grep check (extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in gerrit-verify.yaml)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'extendable from the repo's existing SHA-pin convention in gerrit-verify.yaml' to 'implementing the SHA-pin convention documented in gerrit-verify.yaml:21 (the convention is a comment policy, not an existing automated check — this AC creates the first automated enforcement)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
